Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard VCS (git) source retrieval from the legitimate upstream repository at https://github.com/awesomeWM/awesome.git, which aligns with AUR packaging norms for -git packages. The `md5sums=('SKIP')` entry is expected and safe for git sources, as checksums are not applicable to mutable git repos. The `pkgver()` function changes into the checked-out git directory and runs `git describe` to generate a version string, which is a common and safe practice for -git packages.

The commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only source the PKGBUILD up to the point of fetching the git repository and evaluating `pkgver()`. This does not execute any build() or package() logic. The `pkgver()` function contains no obfuscated code, network access, or data exfiltration. It only runs `git describe` locally on the cloned repository to format the version string. All dependencies, sources, and variables are consistent with packaging expectations for awesome-git.

While `_BUILD_DOCS` is used as an environment variable toggle, it only controls CMake arguments and is not leveraged in a dangerous way during source retrieval or parsing. There are no top-level command substitutions, eval statements, or remote code inclusions outside of declared `source` entries. The source URL is appropriate and matches the project's official homepage.
</details>
<summary>
SAFE: Standard -git PKGBUILD with no malicious fetch-time behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- SAFE: Standard -git PKGBUILD with no malicious fetch-time behavior[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard Arch User Repository (AUR) package for the <pkgname>awesome-git</pkgname>, a development version of the awesome window manager. It fetches the source from the official GitHub repository (https://github.com/awesomeWM/awesome.git) using the `git+` protocol, which is expected and safe for -git packages. The `md5sums=('SKIP')` is appropriate for VCS sources, as the content is mutable and checksums cannot be pinned.

The build process uses standard CMake commands to compile the code and install it into `$pkgdir`. The package installs the window manager, includes proper metadata, and places the X session file in the correct location. No obfuscated code, suspicious downloads, or dangerous runtime modifications (e.g., `eval`, `curl|bash`, `wget`, or remote execution) are present. All dependencies and configuration files are consistent with the application's purpose as a window manager.

The script defines a `pkgver()` function that parses git tags from the repository, which is normal for -git packages. Optional documentation building is controlled via an environment variable but does not introduce risk. No unexpected network calls, file operations, or post-install hooks that could lead to privilege escalation or data exfiltration are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR package with safe VCS build practices</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for awesomerc.lua:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `awesomerc.lua` file is a configuration script for the Awesome window manager. It defines standard client rules, keybindings, widgets, layouts, and appearance settings consistent with typical AwesomeWM configuration practices. The code loads core modules like `awful`, `gears`, `wibox`, and `naughty`, sets up menus, tags, taskbars, and applies themes—all of which are standard and benign operations.

One notable line includes `exe_callback = awful.util.eval` in a keybinding that allows execution of Lua code via a prompt. While `awful.util.eval` can execute arbitrary Lua code, this functionality is part of AwesomeWM's built-in features and is only accessible to the logged-in user via a deliberate keybinding (Mod4 + x). This is not a supply-chain attack vector but rather an expected feature for advanced users to extend window manager behavior at runtime.

There are no signs of malicious activity such as network exfiltration, unauthorized file access, code obfuscation, or remote code execution. The file does not spawn external processes beyond standard user-configured applications like `xterm` or `nano`. All behaviors align with legitimate AwesomeWM customization.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AwesomeWM configuration, no malicious code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for CMakeLists.txt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided CMakeLists.txt file is a standard build configuration script for the Awesome window manager. It defines source files, include directories, compilation flags, custom targets for generating documentation, manpages, icons, and version information, as well as installation and testing procedures. All operations are confined to the build and source directories, and no external network requests, file exfiltration, or execution of untrusted code is present.

The script uses only expected build-time tools (e.g., `asciidoctor`, `gzip`, `convert`, `busted`, `luacheck`) and invokes local scripts from the `${SOURCE_DIR}/build-utils/` directory (such as `git-version-stamp.sh`, `dist.sh`, `check_for_invalid_requires.lua`). These are part of the normal development workflow and consistent with upstream practices. There is no obfuscation, suspicious payload generation, or unauthorized system access.

Custom commands and targets serve legitimate purposes: generating manpages, processing icons, running tests, and ensuring out-of-source builds. Dependencies are explicitly declared, and all file operations are scoped within the project tree. The script adheres to standard CMake and software packaging practices, with no evidence of supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Standard build script, no malicious activity detected.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for check_for_invalid_requires.lua:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Lua script `check_for_invalid_requires.lua` is a static analysis tool designed to validate module dependencies in a Lua codebase, specifically for the awesome window manager (as implied by modules like `awful`, `wibox`, `gears`, and `beautiful`). It uses the optional `depgraph` library to parse require() calls and checks them against a whitelist of allowed dependencies to prevent cycles and enforce architectural boundaries.

The script does not perform any network operations, file system modifications, or execution of external commands. It safely handles the absence of the `depgraph` module by issuing a non-fatal error message and exiting normally. The logic consists solely of string manipulation and table lookups to enforce dependency rules within the application's module hierarchy. There is no obfuscated code, no data exfiltration, and no unexpected system interactions.

All functionality aligns with standard development tooling used in software projects to maintain code quality and structure. The script poses no security risk and does not introduce any supply-chain threats.
</details>
<summary>
Static analysis script, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

It does not execute network requests, invoke dangerous commands (like eval, curl, or wget), modify system files, or perform any suspicious file operations outside the scope of generating source code. LLM auditresponse for AutoOption.cmake:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file <AutoOption.cmake> is a CMake utility script designed to streamline option management in build configurations. It defines two functions: `autoOption` for creating configurable options with an "auto" default state, and `autoDisable` for conditionally disabling such options based on dependency checks. This is standard practice in CMake projects to support flexible and portable build systems.

The code does not perform any network operations, file exfiltration, code evaluation, or system modifications. It uses standard CMake commands (e.g., `set`, `message`, `set_property`) for configuration-time logic and contains no obfuscation, `eval`-like constructs, or shell command execution. The presence of comments describing usage further suggests it is a legitimate developer utility.

No malicious behavior is present. The script operates entirely within the build system context and adheres to expected CMake idioms. Its functionality supports conditional compilation and is consistent with normal, safe build scripting practices.
</details>
<summary>
CMake utility script with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for Makefile:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The Makefile performs standard build automation tasks using CMake and Make, which are common and safe practices for C/C++ projects. It defines typical targets such as building in a subdirectory (`cmake-build`), installing (`install`), cleaning (`distclean`), and generating tags (`tags`). All commands operate locally and use predictable paths: it creates a `build` directory, runs `cmake` from source, and forwards make targets to the build subdirectory. There are no network operations, use of dangerous functions (like `curl|bash`), file exfiltration, or execution of downloaded content.

The Makefile respects the CMAKE\_ARGS variable for user-defined CMake configuration and suppresses verbose output by default unless `VERBOSE` is set. All operations are transparent and consistent with standard build systems. The `tags` target uses `git ls-files` and `ctags`, which is a normal developer workflow enhancement for code navigation. No suspicious obfuscation, unexpected system modifications, or privilege escalations are present.

This file does not pose a supply-chain threat. It acts solely as a wrapper around CMake, which is a normal and safe pattern in AUR packages.
</details>
<summary>
Standard build wrapper, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for atoms-int.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `atoms-int.sh` is a simple shell script that reads a list of atom names from a file (provided as an argument) and generates C code declarations and initializations for XCB atom constants. It outputs a comment, declares variables of type `xcb_atom_t` for each atom, and then builds a static array of `atom_item_t` structures that store the atom name, length, and reference to the corresponding variable.

The script performs no network operations, does not execute external payloads, and does not use obfuscated or dangerous commands. All operations are limited to reading input and generating static C declarations, which is consistent with code-generation practices in build systems. There are no signs of data exfiltration, unauthorized file access, or execution of untrusted code.

The use of `read` and `echo` is standard for shell-based code generators, and the script does not invoke `eval`, `curl`, or other high-risk utilities. The shebang and vim modeline are benign and common in source repositories.
</details>
<summary>
Harmless C code generator, no malicious activity</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for awesome.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a standard `.desktop` file used by desktop environments to register the awesome window manager as a launchable application. It contains only expected fields: `Name`, `Comment`, `TryExec`, `Exec`, and `Type`, all pointing to the `awesome` binary. These fields are typical for such files and do not introduce any security risk.

There is no executable code, network activity, file manipulation, or system modification performed by this file. It is a static configuration file used solely for desktop integration. The `Exec` and `TryExec` entries reference a local binary, which is standard practice and does not indicate malicious behavior.

The file does not contain obfuscated commands, unauthorized data exfiltration, or any form of injected malicious logic. Its content aligns fully with the intended functionality of a desktop entry for a window manager.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry, no security issues
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for git-version-stamp.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `git-version-stamp.sh` is a utility for updating version strings in source files based on `git describe --dirty` output. It reads a previous version stamp from a file (`$1`), compares it with the current Git description, and if different, updates both the target file (`$2`) and the stamp file with the new version using `sed` and `mv`. All operations are local and limited to version string substitution within the build environment.

There are no network operations, no code execution (such as `eval`, `curl|bash`, or `wget`), no obfuscation, and no file manipulations outside the provided paths. The use of `sed` to replace a version string is standard in build systems. The script does not exfiltrate data or modify system files. The `die` function logs warnings but does not halt the build, which is a usability choice, not a security issue.

This script supports reproducible builds by ensuring version metadata reflects the current Git state. It operates within expected boundaries of AUR packaging tooling and poses no supply-chain risk.
</details>
<summary>
Local version stamp update; no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[9/10] Reviewing src/awesome-git/build-utils/dist.sh...
+ Reviewed src/awesome-git/build-utils/git-version-stamp.sh. Status: SAFE -- Local version stamp update; no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for dist.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `dist.sh` is a release packaging script used to create source distributions of the awesome window manager from a specified Git tag. It performs standard operations such as git archive, tar compression (bzip2 and xz), and GPG signing of the resulting archives. The script extracts the commit date from the Git repository to ensure reproducible timestamps and creates a `.version_stamp` file inside the archive to record the exact Git tag used.

All operations are performed locally using trusted Git revision data, and the outputs are source archives and their cryptographic signatures. The script does not download or execute remote code, exfiltrate data, or perform any network operations. Use of `gpg --detach-sign` is standard practice for source distribution integrity and authenticity.

The script assumes the Git tag provided as input is legitimate, but this is a normal trust boundary in release tooling. No obfuscation, dangerous eval patterns, or unexpected file system manipulations are present. The actions are consistent with upstream release procedures.
</details>
<summary>
Standard release script with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for check_for_invalid_requires.lua:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a static analysis script that enforces dependency rules within the Awesome WM codebase. It reads local Lua source files from a "lib" directory, builds a dependency graph using the `depgraph` library, and validates that all `require()` calls are whitelisted in the `allowed_deps` table. The purpose is to prevent cyclic dependencies and maintain a clean module structure. There is no network activity, no execution of untrusted code, no obfuscation, and no system modification. The script exits gracefully (return code 0) if the `depgraph` library is unavailable, which is a routine robustness measure. This is a standard development tool, not a supply-chain attack.
</details>
<summary>
Legitimate dependency validation script; no security risks.</summary>
</security_assessment>

This Makefile is a standard top-level wrapper for a CMake-based project (typical for AUR packages like awesome-git). It creates a build directory, runs `cmake` with provided arguments, then delegates build and install commands to the generated `Makefile` inside that directory. Additional targets (`tags`, `distclean`) are routine developer conveniences.  

There are no network requests, no downloads from external or unexpected sources, no obfuscated or encoded commands, and no attempts to exfiltrate data or modify system files outside the package's scope. The only invocation of `git` is in the `tags` target (`git ls-files | xargs ctags`), which operates entirely on the local repository and is a standard practice for generating code navigation tags. All operations are confined to expected build and install workflows.
</details>

</security_assessment>

<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `awesomeConfig.cmake` is a standard CMake configuration file for the Awesome window manager (awesome-git). It performs routine build setup tasks: version detection (via `git describe` or stamp file), library discovery via `pkg_check_modules` and `find_program`, optional feature toggles, file generation, and custom build targets for documentation and Lua files.  

No obfuscated code, suspicious network requests (all external references are standard upstream libraries from pkg-config), or dangerous commands (eval, base64, curl, wget) are present. The only `execute_process` call runs a version check on a local tool (`ldoc`), which is harmless. All file operations (`configure_file`, `file(WRITE/READ)`, `copy`) operate within the build directory and source tree, not on system files. There is no exfiltration, backdoor, or injection of untrusted code. The file follows normal packaging practices for a -git package.  

Thus, the file is safe.
</details>
<summary>Standard CMake build config, no malicious behavior.</summary>
